text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic diaphragmatic hernia repair, positioning a surgical mesh over the defect in the diaphragm to reinforce the area.
New Hanover Regional Medical Center, Department of Surgery. Wilmington, North Carolina.
A forty-nine year old female presents with right flank pain for several days. She first noted the pain after sexual intercourse.
She denies vomiting but endorses nausea. She denies changes in bowel movements.
No chest pain or shortness of breath. She denies previous surgery or history of trauma.
Preoperative imaging revealed large right posterior diaphragmatic hernia with colon in the thoracic cavity.
The patient was brought to the operating room table. General anesthesia was induced. She was positioned

In the lateral decubitus position with the left side down, the surgeon explores the posterior aspect of the right diaphragm, revealing a hernia defect. A large amount of omentum and colon is visible in the right chest cavity, which is successfully reduced.
The surgical exploration reveals a hernia defect measuring seven by five centimeters in the posterior aspect of the right diaphragm.
The surgeon achieves complete visualization of the right chest cavity, noting the absence of a peritoneal hernia sac, allowing clear observation of the anatomical structures within the cavity.
The surgeon mobilizes the right lobe of the liver, likely to improve access to the hernia defect in the right diaphragm.
The surgeon performs retroperitoneal dissection to further expose the left lower aspect of the diaphragmatic defect, medially reflecting the right kidney.
The surgeon closes the diaphragmatic defect using figure-of-eight permanent sutures, starting from the medial side and progressing laterally towards the chest wall, utilizing an endostitch device.
The surgeon uses a suture passer to close the diaphragmatic defect laterally under increased tension. A coated propylene mesh is then placed over the entire defect and secured with tacks.
The surgeon allows the kidney and liver to return to their normal anatomical positions, resulting in the majority of the previously placed mesh being covered by these organs.
The patient did well postoperatively. She was discharged home on postoperative day one in stable condition.
She returned to the hospital one month later with cold cystitis.
At that time, a CT scan was obtained. Here you can see that there is no residual hernia defect.
The preoperative CAT scan is on the left. The postoperative CAT scan is on the right.
